# The Architecture 2.0 Lifecycle {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979) [@Box1979Robustness]
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box coined this aphorism about statistical models. Every model simplifies, and the working question is whether the simplification still serves the decision at hand. His point applies to architecture: a task description, benchmark, simulator, power model, or test suite always leaves something out. Each can still be useful when its scope and failure modes are clear.
:::


::: {.callout-crux}
How do we structure our hypotheses, constraints, and toolchains so that an AI agent can execute an architecture study without silently violating the physical and logical realities of the system?
:::

The preceding chapters established a hard reality: modern architecture spaces have grown too complex for us to explore manually, yet unguided AI risks optimizing proxy metrics at the expense of physical silicon constraints. We cannot simply hand over a prompt and expect a taped-out chip in return. To safely delegate exploration to an AI agent, the tacit human knowledge we naturally carry—such as implicit workload expectations, unstated thermal limits, and trusted tool combinations—must become explicit, machine-readable state.

This requires a formal ontology: a conceptual model of our work that strictly defines what an agent can see, what it can change, and how we will judge its results. By formalizing our intent and constraints, we turn stochastic models from autonomous novelties into accountable participants in our design loop.

This chapter establishes the Architecture 2.0 lifecycle, a structured engineering discipline for AI-assisted architecture. We establish the bounded architecture study as the fundamental unit of work, ensuring that every AI-driven exploration remains anchored to a testable claim. We then introduce the six-stage lifecycle that carries this claim from formulation to defense, ensuring our design loop remains inspectable, rigorous, and firmly under our control.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After reading this chapter, you can:

- Formulate bounded architecture studies by specifying explicit design-space constraints, baseline workloads, and verifiable claims.
- Structure multi-fidelity design-space exploration using explicit escalation policies and standardized tool interfaces.
- Differentiate genuine architectural improvements from surrogate model artifacts by applying causal simulator ablations.
- Establish formal decision-rights matrices and design-loop cards to partition automated agentic execution from non-delegable human sign-off authority.
:::

## The Big Picture: Translating Intent to Execution {#sec-arch2-ontology}

An AI-assisted architecture study needs a common structure connecting the stages of work that are often documented separately. It must preserve our original intent, define a bounded comparison, and connect that comparison to an inspectable execution loop.

@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl places an architecture study between our question and the tools used to investigate it. The figure's middle layer is the comparison and its review record. It connects reasoning that often stays in our heads to executable implementation flows, giving architects and AI agents a shared context.

![**An AI-assisted architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence.** The study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis process, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="85%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing architecture intent and judgment flowing into a structured architecture study, with tool results flowing back for evidence review."}

Read @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl from top to bottom:

* **The Upper Layer (System Intent)**: This contains the architecture models, requirements, and rationale that shape our question. For the RISC-V XR subsystem from our Lighthouse prompt, this includes the high-level goal of improving mobile XR efficiency.
* **The Middle Layer (Bounded Comparison)**: This turns intent into a bounded comparison. Its scope names the specific question, such as deciding whether a particular cache configuration merits RTL evaluation. The architectural claim says what will be reviewed. It connects design intent to tool execution.
* **The Lower Layer (Executable Tool Path)**: Simulators, compilers, RTL generators, and EDA flows carry out different parts of the comparison to return measurements that enter the evidence record.

In software engineering, coding agents rely on structured environments like SWE-bench to provide a bounded issue, a known repository state, and executable tests. Architecture requires a similar structure, but building it is much harder because feedback (power, area, timing) is delayed, ambiguous, and subject to physical constraints.

## The Six-Stage Execution Lifecycle

Experienced architects already move through these layers informally. When we run the work ourselves, we naturally carry project context from a spreadsheet to a simulator to a design review. An AI agent cannot. To safely integrate AI, we must make this implicit workflow explicit. Without a formalized loop, an agent might optimize a proxy metric while silently violating a physical constraint we knew about but forgot to write down.

As shown in @fig-ontology-loop, this structured approach maps our familiar architecture design process into a six-stage lifecycle: Formulate, Explore, Implement, Evaluate, Explain, and Defend.

![**The Architecture 2.0 engineering lifecycle.** Six numbered stages carry one turn of an architecture study from a bounded claim through search, tool execution, evidence, and interpretation to an authorized decision. A turn that fails review returns a revised claim or study to Formulate rather than stopping.](images/ch3-ontology-loop){#fig-ontology-loop width="100%" fig-alt="Six numbered rectangular boxes in two rows. The top row runs left to right through Formulate, Explore, and Implement; the bottom row runs right to left through Evaluate, Explain, and Defend. A red return path carries a revised claim or study from Defend back to Formulate for another turn."}

> **Architecture design loop.** The repeated process of examining the current design, making an allowed change, running the relevant tools, interpreting the result, and deciding whether to revise, reject, or continue. Each iteration retains enough information for another architect to see what led to the next step.

The design loop is the execution mechanism inside a bounded architecture study. The rest of this chapter walks through each of these six stages, showing how we orchestrate AI agents safely and rigorously.

## Formulating: Bounding the Study

The first step in the lifecycle (@fig-ontology-loop) is Formulate. To ensure that machine-generated proposals do not drift away from the problem they were asked to solve, we require a bounded architecture study.

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, the architecture question and the judgment behind it establish the starting intent. The Lighthouse prompt states that intent:

> *Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload...*

The system intent is to improve mobile XR efficiency. But this is not yet an architecture task. An AI system given only the product intent cannot know which comparisons the architect needs. The intent must be translated into a claim, a baseline, and a search space.

### Defining the Claim

A bounded study anchors everything to an architectural claim: a falsifiable statement that the review process will eventually evaluate.
For example, "Increasing the L2 cache associativity to 8-way will reduce XRBench latency by 10% without exceeding the 3 W budget."

The claim tells the AI agent what to optimize and tells the human reviewer what to scrutinize. Without a claim, an agent might endlessly tune parameters without a stopping condition, or it might propose a massive cache that maximizes IPC but physically melts the chip.

### Defining the Search Space

Once the claim is stated, the architect must bound the design space. This means explicitly defining the legal parameters, the baseline configuration, and the strict constraints. If the agent is only allowed to modify cache parameters, it cannot rewrite the out-of-order execution core.

These boundaries prevent the agent from wandering into unverified regions of the architecture where simulators might fail silently or produce physically impossible results.

## Exploring: State Representation and Search

With the problem formulated, the next stage is Explore. In this stage, an AI agent searches the bounded design space to find promising architecture candidates.

However, an agent cannot explore a space it cannot see or understand. For the search to be effective, we must explicitly provide the agent with a machine-readable view of the architecture and a way to quickly predict the consequences of its choices before running expensive simulations.

### State Representation

An AI agent can act only on the information we make available to it. For human architects, design knowledge lives in natural-language specifications, ISA manuals, spreadsheet models, and meeting notes. An agent requires this tacit knowledge to be translated into explicit, represented state. This state must capture the current workload, the baseline design, our interface assumptions, and any results from earlier iterations.

When an agent proposes an impossible design, the root cause is often missing state representation, not a flawed search algorithm.

### Prediction and Generating Candidates

Running a full cycle-accurate simulation or RTL synthesis for every single candidate is too slow. Instead, the agent relies on prediction models (fast proxies) to estimate the performance, power, and area (PPA) impacts of a change. These proxies act as the agent's internal "world model" [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels], allowing it to screen out bad ideas before committing to a costly tool execution.

Guided by its view of the state and its internal predictions, the agent generates candidates. For highly constrained problems, it might tune numerical parameters using Bayesian optimization. For more open-ended exploration, it might use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate new structural topologies. Because LLMs are prone to hallucinating invalid syntax, this generation step requires constructive heuristics to ensure every proposed candidate is syntactically legal before it is ever sent to a simulator.

## Implementing: Toolchain Friction and Escalation

Moving to the Implement stage (@fig-ontology-loop), once the agent proposes a candidate architecture, the next step is executing the simulation and compilation tools to gather physical evidence.

### Executing the Design Loop

The interaction between the agent's proposed candidates and the underlying tools forms a continuous execution cycle. @fig-design-loop expands this process into one iteration. An architect or AI method reads the recorded state, makes an allowed change, and runs the relevant tool.

![**How an iterative architecture study proceeds.** An architect or AI method reads the recorded design state, makes an allowed change, runs a tool, and submits the result for review. The reviewer records what the evidence supports, and the decision owner determines what happens next.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="85%" fig-alt="A design loop within a scoped architecture study. Represented state, permitted action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review."}

### Navigating Toolchain Friction

Unlike software environments where code compiles in milliseconds, architecture tools are often fragile, slow, and prone to silent failures. An AI interacting with these tools will encounter licensing timeouts, parsing errors, and out-of-memory crashes.

To operate effectively, an agent needs clear tool interfaces (like the ArchGym API [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]) that expose not just the success path, but also the failure modes and recovery actions. A bounded study turns toolchain friction from a fatal obstacle into a recorded constraint. When an agent cannot evaluate a candidate due to a tool crash, it logs the failure as negative evidence and pivots.

### Multi-Fidelity Escalation

Bounded architecture studies span multiple levels of abstraction, from fast analytical proxies to cycle-accurate simulators and slow RTL synthesis.

The challenge arises when an agent optimizing against a fast proxy encounters a boundary condition where the proxy's assumptions break down. If the agent quietly trusts the low-fidelity result without validating it against a higher-fidelity tool, it risks generating configurations that fail physical constraints.

> **Multi-fidelity escalation.** A defined policy that states the conditions under which an agent must transition from a fast proxy to a high-fidelity tool [@KandasamyEtAl2017MultiFidelityBO]. It specifies the budget for high-fidelity runs and the checks required before escalation.

## Evaluating and Explaining: Evidence vs Feedback

In the Evaluate stage, we assess the results. The study must distinguish raw tool feedback from reviewable architectural evidence.

### Feedback vs. Reviewable Evidence

When an AI agent changes a parameter and a tool completes the run, the result is *feedback*. It becomes *evidence* only when it is tied to the architectural claim and the run record identifies the conditions, constraints, and provenance under which it was produced.

The same power number supports different decisions depending on whether it came from a proxy, cycle-level simulation, synthesis, or silicon. Recording the provenance is what lets another architect verify a reported number.

### The Value of Negative Results

Rejected candidates, failed simulator runs, and proxy wins that disappear at higher fidelity all help mathematically map the valid design space. An AI agent shouldn't simply discard these failures; it must preserve them as negative evidence to prevent future agents from wasting expensive tool runs on known dead ends.

### The "Clever Hans" Risk and Causal Ablation

In the Explain stage, we provide a mechanistic account of the results. A fast proxy score tells us *what* happened, but the explanation stage tells us *why*.

When an AI agent proposes a design change and a simulator confirms it runs faster, the agent must provide a falsifiable architectural mechanism. Did the change actually reduce conflict misses, or did the AI simply find a way to skip a mandatory initialization routine? Without demanding a mechanism, the architect risks accepting a "Clever Hans" result [@LapuschkinEtAl2019CleverHans].

The explanation stage mitigates this risk by isolating the causal mechanism through targeted ablations and counterfactual simulations. While AI techniques like SHAP explain the *AI's proxy* predictions, this must be explicitly contrasted with ground-truth simulator ablation, which requires parameter-toggling in the actual simulator.

## Defending: Authority and the Design-Loop Card

No single stage operates in a vacuum. The Defend stage (@fig-ontology-loop) forces the loop to surface its claims and its mechanistic explanations for explicit sign-off. It is here that the system distinguishes between the agent's autonomous execution and the architect's non-delegable authority.

### Action vs. Authority

Describing a system as autonomous does not specify what it is allowed to do. Architects should assign separate decision rights instead of giving the system one overall level of automation.

An AI method can hold real initiative over proposing candidates, executing tool actions, and rejecting a candidate when a declared check fails, while the rights to waive a check or commit resources stay with the named people who answer for the outcome.

### The Design-Loop Card

With authority assigned and the evidence reviewed, one need remains. Another architect must be able to take in a whole bounded study at a glance.

> **Design-loop card.** A short guide to one architecture study. It points to the question, scope, and claim, then shows how architects and AI agents carried out the work. Detailed design state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

The ML community adopted a similar practice with model cards to make reporting more transparent [@MitchellEtAl2019ModelCards]. Just as model cards disclose evaluation data to prevent misuse of a trained model, the design-loop card discloses the bounds and limits of an architectural comparison (@fig-design-loop-card-example).

![**A design-loop card indexes one architecture study.** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution process, evidence, and decision owner.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="85%" fig-alt="A compact study index card for the Lighthouse prompt, listing fields from intent through failed runs."}

A design-loop card cannot settle every dispute, but it can show a later reviewer which records a contested result actually disclosed. The card helps reviewers locate missing scope or evidence, preventing the name or capability of an AI agent from becoming a substitute for adequate architectural evidence.

## Conclusion

The Architecture 2.0 lifecycle gives an AI agent real initiative inside the loop while keeping the checks that verify its work, the claim it is tested against, and the final authority outside its reach. By rigorously formalizing how we formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend our decisions, we transform AI from a stochastic novelty into a trusted participant in the engineering of computer architecture.
